reading_logs.notes: Shaykh Mahmud's three attempts to visit the Blessed Beauty; Abdu'r-Rahman Pasha's attempt to close Baha'i shops in Akka
Roy Wilhelm taught Martha the Faith in 1908

Martha met Thornton Chase, the first American Baha'i,  in a restaurant. They talked for several hours, and she later said that their talk was a stepping stone to her accepting the Faith, which she did in 1909; 1898 was the first pilgrimage for believers from the West; Thornton Chase met 'Abdu'l-Baha in 1907; 1909 was the first National Convention for the National Spiritual Assembly of the United States and Canada; Martha was a delegate for the Naitonal Convention from 1911 onwards (when she was in the country); The Young Turk Revolution was in 1908, had to free all political and religious prisoners which led to freedom of 'Abdu'l-Baha

Started her first trip around the world on 30 January 1915; On 24 April 1915 she sailed for India, landing in Bombay; Martha was the first American Baha'i to visit Burma; She met Siyyid Mustafa Rumi in Mandalay, Burma; Agnes Alexander was living as a teacher in Japan, and Martha was there in July 1915; The first Japanese to accept the Faith in Japan is Mr. Fukuta; On 31 July 1915, she sailed out of Yokohama and had a short stay in Manchuria; In 1917 five Tablets of the Divine Plan were read in Boston at the National Convention; In 1917 Roy Wilhelm released two blue teaching booklets, little Ben and Big Ben, which Martha found to be indespensable; In 1917's Boston Convention, Martha and Harry Randall first worked together and their friends called them soulmates; Martha developed a lasting friendship with Agens Parsons while working together on the publicity committee for the 1917 celebration of the centenary of the Birth of Baha'u'llah
She crossed the Andes in 1919; She reached Panama on 25 October 1919

She was helping with the talks of Jinab-i-Fadil in the United States and Canada; She served on the Ideas Committee of the Temple Board; She helped with planning the amity conference with Agnes Parsons, which was supposed to be held in 1920 but was held in the Spring of 1921
She wrote to the President of Mexico, Álvaro Obregón, sharing the 'Economic Solution' as discussed in Star of the West 31 December 1919 in the article by Mr. Alfred E. Lunt
On 25 April 1923 she embarked on a steamer headed for China

She started in Peking; She visited Ginanfu (now Chin ch'eng), and then Chefoo (now Yentai), home of Confucius, then Tsuchowfu, then Nanking, then Shanghai; She wrote articles for the Shanghai Times newspaper
She said "It is a Favour and a Bounty of God to have the pribilege to do anything for China. I do not think one of you will ever come who will not love the Chinese people.";

reading_logs.book_title: Tablets of Bahá’u’lláh revealed after the Kitáb-i-Aqdas

reading_logs.notes: She met the Archbishop of Sweden who was awared the Nobel Peace Prize in 1930 - she gave him the Kitab-i-Iqan and Baha'u'llah and the New Era; She met Queen Marie and her daughter again.

Princess Ileana translated some of the Baha'i writings into Rumanian; She visited the first president of the republic of Czechoslovakia, Thomas Masaryk in 1928 and he encouraged her and supported her efforts

In all the Scandinavian and Baltic countries Martha sensed the vitality and integrity of the northern people. "I tell you," she wrote a few months later, "that the Bahá'i culture of the North may be a rose that blooms later, but when it does bloom it will be fresh, fragrant, exquisite! The flowers of the North pos(s]ess a freshness all their own!" If Bahä'u'lláh'steachings for world peace could be established in Scandinavia, Martha felt, this region would lead the way to the formation of a Universal League of Nations that would unite the nations of the world. Martha was years ahead of her time, as that institution would come into being in 1945 as the United Nations, with its first secretary general, Trygve Lie, of Oslo, Norway.
"In Japan I may only stay two or three weeks. (Japan is very expensive, I can't stay there very long.) I expect to make my long stay in China and I must earn a little by teaching English lessons."

"August 14-The air • • raids came over the Shanghai International Settlement! The whirring, the stacatto [sic] louder and louder.•. over our heads, then a ... [silence] then the horror, the shock, the shaking house and the awful explosions of bursting bombs, it was HELL upon earth!... I was on the fifth floor, the top floor and I thought our house was falling. I was living one-half block from the place they were aiming to bomb and less than two blocks from Palace Hotel and Cathay.. [Hotel] where the bombs crashed... [through] thundering death. I prayed Ya Alláh-El-Mostaghas!* and bowed my head thinking the next one might fall on me. It was doomsday! The mind lives a lifetime in a moment! But the planes soared farther away and the bombing stopped for that time.*"; On 13-14 February 1938, again traveling with Işfándiar Bakhtiari, Martha made her second trip to the Visva-Bharati University of Rabindranath Tagore. She felt deeply privileged to meet, finally, this world figure, this Nobel-prize-winning poet. His humanistic ideas radiated beyond the borders of In-dia, and his views often determined Mahatma Gandhi's ac-tions. In 1912 he had met Abdu'l-Bahá in Chicago and had ever after been impressed with the ideals of the Bahá'i Faith. Someday he hoped that a "Bahá'i Chair of Religion" could be established at his university.
